speaker: interviewer
text: Becoming the leader of Betar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been involved with Betar at all before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how this came about then.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [BACKROUND NOISE] We had-- I joined the Zionist movement in about 1933. I belonged, at that time, to a fraternity-- students' fraternity.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was its name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ivria. I-V-R-I-A. And later on, it was named [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What do those names mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ivria means Hebrew-- the Hebrew Association. And [INAUDIBLE] means the dawn.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- no, no. Ivria-- a Hebrew word in English means Hebrew-- the Hebrew association. And in-- and later on, it was named [INAUDIBLE] in Hebrew, and-- which is dawn, I think, in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, in fact, debated the Zionist issue with friends of mine very, very vividly before 1933. And I had thought that the solution of what was called the Jewish problem would be in assimilation, in-- and in a greater understanding and a progress of humanity towards a-- against prejudice. But, of course, that proved to be nothing but an illusion. And then I remembered those discussions when Hitler came, and I decided I have to change my views. I have to recognize reality. And it's-- and-- and-- and-- and be conscious of my Jewish identity. And I was very much interested in Zionism, but I-- very frankly, I was not too persuaded that the Zionism which the venerable and venerated Professor Weizmann, at the time, preached-- namely, give in to Great Britain and follow Great Britain and don't make much noise, you know, otherwise, it'll get worse-- that that would get us anywhere. And I was afraid that, also-- that the thought of creating a Jewish State-- a majority of Jews, which was a core of [INAUDIBLE]-- that that was getting lost and being diluted. And there I found the Revisionist Movement, led by an inspired and inspiring man, Ze'ev Jabotinsky, who wanted to recreate and a-- and the Zionist ideal of a Jewish majority. [? And he ?] said, we have to be prepared, because the Arabs are not going to let us do that peacefully. And so I became part of the movement of the Betar. And as a result of my Jewish education, since I was, even as a student, well known at the time, I became its leader. That we learned Hebrew-- studied Hebrew. We had-- that was the first thing. And then Hebrew literature, also. We engaged in paramilitary exercises-- practices at the time. And figured that the new generation of Jews would have to add something to being a-- scholars in Talmud and being intellectuals only, that they would have to fight. And as a matter of fact, I intended to go to Israel at that time. That was unfortunately thwarted, because Israel was-- Palestine-- hermetically closed. And I-- so I went on. I had, in 1900-- in 1900-- in 1935, I took my high school leaving examination back to my school. My school education had not been completed. And under-- so I went to school two years under the Nazi regime. I must say that it was very, very sad. And it was a tremendous shock, because overnight, some of the schoolmates that I went with didn't know us anymore. They took-- they dressed in Nazi uniforms, and they didn't want to have anything to do with us. And some of the professors sort of played the same game. Other professors, however-- and I know quite a few-- had a lot of guts and courage and said they didn't agree with everything that Hitler did. So in 1935, I took my high school leaving examination. I got a very good grade. It was in French and History, and I had to give a discourse in French on the French Naturalist [INAUDIBLE] and other French philosophers. And in 1935, right afterwards, I entered the Jewish a seminary-- theological seminary, because I wanted to become a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was also in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Breslau. Breslau had the oldest rabbinical school-- theological seminary in Germany. It had been founded in 1853. And as I said before, I believe, it had the distinction of a staff of professors who made history in-- in the world of-- of Jewish learning. We had-- of course we studied the Talmud, but at the same time, philosophy and psychology. And it was a tremendous experience. As a matter of fact, there was one philosopher that interested me very much and interests me up to this day. That was Baruch Spinoza. And the professor who acquainted us with his teachings also said that, of course, from a Jewish theological standpoint, he was not accepted. But I got fascinated by him, and we had the learned-- the professor Heinemann, Isaac Heinemann. A blessed memory. He then, later on, went to Israel and taught there. And he was a scholarly man. He was at home both in Greek philosophy and the Greek language and in-- of course, in Talmudic-- in the world of the Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me more about your experiences at the seminary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my experiences at the seminary [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were a-- were one of the-- in fact, I would say this-- this seminary-- my attendance at the seminary belongs to one of the most interesting, creative, seminal experiences in my life, because I had at the-- I was exposed to a tremendous variety of scholarly people. And the people that I met, I-- I got together with every day. We got together on Shabbat, too, on other-- on-- on-- on festive occasions. But I also, at the same time, must admit that, after a year and a half, I decided that I was not cut out to become a rabbi, because my-- my view of Judaism is not necessarily the traditional way. And so I decided, I-- while I have remained a Jew in-- with all my life, with all my heart-- I decided I'm not going to become a rabbi. I wanted to study other subjects. And there, at the same time, I had the distinction of becoming one of the two Jewish students admitted to the University of Breslau under the numerus clausus, which is that we had a quota of two out of 20,000. And there I came in touch with a-- professors and-- who were also outstanding in the-- in philosophy, economics. I studied, particularly, the romance languages, philology. I must say that I have-- I encountered very, very little discrimination at the University at that time. Why that was, I don't know. Although we had one professor who taught in Nazi uniform with a swastika. Oh, yes. That was-- that was an honor, you know, at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- they were very, very interesting people that I met and experiences. I remember one seminar that I took in-- was it philosophy? There was one guy who stood up and said he was a student of Protestant theology. He said to the professor, and to the Nazis who were there, I cannot go along with the race-- racist theory. This is completely counter to what I was taught as a Christian. And then we walked home. I said, are you crazy to say that? He said-- he said, I cannot live a lie and be subjected to something that I don't believe in. There were some people like that, too. And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He had tremendous courage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1937, 1936. Now, you must understand that-- [INAUDIBLE] that the Nazis were-- proceeded gradually. The noose got tighter and tighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of the steps they used to tighten that noose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1935, the so-called Nuremberg Laws-- N-U-R-E-M-B-E-R-G laws-- were promulgated by Hitler. They officially made second or third class citizens out of the Jews. It prohibited the Jews from attending public schools, from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] any other activities in-- professional activities in Germany. It also-- and that was the-- the demoralization, what the Nazis thought would become-- would demoralize the Jews-- it prohibited even the Jews from having a Christian maid at home. It prohibited any marriage between Christians and-- non-Jews and Jews, and said that the Jews no longer [BACKROUND NOISE] had the right to vote. They are just outcasts. They-- as long as they're in Germany, they have German passports-- because, obviously, they must belong to a nationality. But in every other respect, they were pariahs. In 1936, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- well, I remember one-- one situation-- one experience that was shattering. When I-- I went to school-- we once were the school-- we had to attend a Nazi exhibit of-- which wanted to illustrate the racial theory to everybody. And they had typical-- what they thought was typical picture there of the Aryan race-- example of the Aryan race. There was Goethe, and there was a-- a Mozart, and Beethoven, although Beethoven was, of course, of Slavic origin. And then they had a Jew, and they said these are the inferior races. We Jews had to be there, and listen to all that nonsense. But, anyway, back to what we were discussing. In 1936, the Nazis relented a little bit because, at that time, we had the Olympiad-- the Olympic games in Berlin. And Hitler had invited everybody. And for a time-- but at least for a-- a few months-- Germany became a Potemkin village, you know. Everything was disguised. Everybody was welcome, of course, except Joe Lewis. I don't think Hitler shook hands with him at the time. But then it started again in 1937. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Nazis had, in the meantime, set up concentration camps. First, they had only-- they got the-- their opponents. They-- they sent their opponents to concentration camps and Jews who had been prominent in-- as members of the democratic parties-- democratic movement. And then they tried to also cut the Jews off from economic activities. They brought pressure on people, like my dad, too-- they brought pressure on people-- to-- to Jews to sell their business enterprises. And if they didn't want to, other means [BACKROUND NOISE] were employed. They, of course, the sale was nothing but a sham. Now, in 1938--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father did sell his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- my father was a sales representative, so he-- he actually lived off of his commission. But the Nazis already made it very unpleasant for Jews to visit non-Jewish business places. And Jews were not allowed, for instance, certain places. In restaurants, there were big signs. Also, in certain parks. We were just treated like, I would say-- sometimes I would say worse than animals. And I formed the idea I want to get out. I had this-- at that time I went to the university, but I figured this is not going to last much longer, because there's-- there is no future in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you at the stage where you had to wear the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. That did not really start before my immigration. That did not start in Germany until 1939, 1940. And I left Germany in December, 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was 1937, '38. And I was-- while I was studying at the university, I was trying to establish contact with the outside world and see where I could go. And that was very difficult, because the outside world started, already, closing their borders to Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: The United States had a quota system, which made it absolutely illusory for any Jew to try to go there, unless he had affidavits of support-- financial guarantees from people here. So in-- when things got very tough, I remember in-- oh, I had, in 1938-- 1938, the Nazis already started molesting Jews physically. They not only discriminated against them in their businesses and professions, but they made it practically impossible for them to go on. My father had to give up all of his financial-- all of his business activities. And, as far as I'm concerned, I went-- I went to Berlin and went to different consulates, trying to get visas. That was very difficult. It proved to be-- it proved to be impossible. I remember, in summer 1938, I was a-- I went to a little seaside resort, and some Nazi told me to get out of the water or he would shoot me. Because, of course, he could recognize me as a Jew. There were other incidents that told me that I have to get out in a hurry. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any of them come to mind right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was another incident. In fact, I was at the beach at a place by the name of [INAUDIBLE] in Germany. That's on the Baltic Sea. And I was sitting there and reading a book. And all of a sudden, a woman came by and said, oh, it's-- it's nice weather here, and so on and so forth. And I said, yes, it's nice. And she kept going.

speaker: interviewee
text: She left, and about an-- half an hour later, two Nazis in uniforms approached me and said, what is your name? And I gave them my name. They said, well, there's a woman who accused you of molesting her. I said, what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, yes, well, I-- you have to come with us. And one said, well, you know, we have Buchenwald, and we have other concentration camps, et cetera, et cetera. To my fortune-- to my good luck, they-- they called another man, who was an elderly man. He didn't believe them. And he said, we want to get that woman here and find out what she has to say. They couldn't find her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was released. And then, from there on, I went to Berlin. And I again tried each and every consulate, and I couldn't. I-- I just was unable, except to get any visa. There was one country, and that was Haiti, where-- which a-- whose consulate in Berlin offered me a visa. However, that was difficult, because I found out that there was absolutely no opportunity in Haiti to do anything-- to engage in any gainful activity. Then came November, 1938. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Kristallnacht. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On the 9th of November, 1938, I-- we-- we got a call. A friend called and said, the synagogue is on fire. They are looting Jewish stores. I-- despite everything that had gone on, it's-- it was hard to believe until you made sure, and I made sure. My-- I remember my father, who left for his office, he said, I want to see what's going on. That was the last time I saw him. I, myself, took the street car and went downtown. And I saw Nazis standing in front of Jewish stores. People looting Jewish stores. I-- I went to the synagogue, and there I saw the flames shoot out of the beautiful dome of the synagogue. The night before, I had gone to the Jewish library, and I tried to take out three books. They told me, you could only take out one book, and that was [INAUDIBLE]. I saved it from the flames, and I have it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you go to the library the day before Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simply because I kept-- I borrowed books from the-- from library. I wanted to have-- there were certain-- there was certain research that I was doing with the-- one of the books of-- of the [INAUDIBLE]. And I just wanted to check that out, and there were two other books-- history books-- which I couldn't take, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewee
text: The morning-- then the morning-- back to the morning of the 9th of November-- I got back. I was shattered. I took back the street car back, and I heard people say, well, why don't they throw all the Jews into the temple there? It was a nightmare. When I got off the street car, I was on my way home. I took-- I wanted to call my house. And all of a sudden, I noticed somebody behind me. It was my brother-in-law at the time. He said, we can't stay here. I said, well, what do you want to do? He said, let's take the train to Berlin, because otherwise they will arrest us. I went home, told my mother that we wanted to go to Berlin. I said, have you heard from my father? She said, no. But you go. So we went. We walked to the railroad station. On the way, we met a mob surrounding a number of Jews, who were led on by the Gestapo, who had been arrested in the street. People-- the Nazis arrested Jews wherever they could find them.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked with our suitcases. And I would say one of the miracles of my life is that they did not arrest me. I walked right past them. The jeering mob, the jeering crowd. And about-- there must have been at least 50 Jewish people-- I knew quite a few of them-- who were driven to the railroad station from where they took the train to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the concentration camps-- Buchenwald, as I found out later.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took the train-- the last train from Breslau to Berlin that was not searched, raided by the Gestapo. On the train was a chief rabbi of Breslau. And I said, where are you going? He said, I'm going to Hamburg to take the boat to the United States. My brother-in-law and I got off in Berlin, and there was a-- I said, where are we going to go? Well, across the-- across the street, there was a huge hotel with a swastika. And I said, you know, I've been in that hotel about three months before. So we went in there, and I said, can you accommodate me? The lady says, oh, well, I will take you. You know, I--I fly the swastika here. Nobody's going to think that you are here. So I called from there, and I called my relatives. I had relatives in Berlin, and one of my aunts said, why don't you come and stay with me? In order not to be found out and arrested by the Gestapo, we went to a movie house in Berlin. Can you imagine? I still remember the movie that we saw about Bengali-- B-E-N-G-A-L-I-- a British movie. We got out. It was dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked down the main street of West Berlin, [GERMAN], just like Fifth Avenue. And there had been the beautiful Jewish-- A beautiful temple, Fasanenstrasse, F-A-S-A-N-E-N-S-T-R-A-S-S-E, which was one of the pride of the Jewish community in Berlin. It was in ashes. And I still remember an old man-- not-- a non-Jew, it must have been-- I'm sure he was not Jewish-- with a cane, standing in front of that-- the ashes-- glimmering ashes, shaking his head and said, what a shame for Germany. Well, I stayed with my aunt. I had two aunts in Berlin. And she did not live too far from the German Reich Chancellery, on Wilhelmstrasse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to call my home, but from a public phone so they wouldn't find out who-- where I was. Finally, [BACKROUND NOISE] one day, I was called by a relative of mine in Berlin, my aunt who lived in Spandau-- S-P-A-N-D-A-U. She said, I have to talk to you, but you-- I must ask you to summon up all your strength. I knew that something-- some terrible news was in store for me. She said-- she was already-- she was in her 70s. She said, you need all your [NON-ENGLISH] that you can summon up. Your father has been killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, where? We understand in Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all I can tell you is they say it was a heart attack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, to say the least, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was completely incomprehensible to me. As we found out later, my father had been asked to present himself to the police. My [? mother-- ?] he-- when he came home, my mother said, the police have been here. They want you. He said, well, I have not done anything criminal. I'm not worried about it. So he went to the police. He thought-- he believed in law and order. That was the mentality of a lot of German Jews. We haven't done anything, so what can they do to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The police sent him to the gathering station-- the depot in Breslau where he was shipped to-- from where he was shipped, with hundreds of others, to Buchenwald. In Buchenwald, they killed him. I have eyewitnesses who live in the United States who were present. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He collapsed, and so they killed him. And a few days later, my mother, who didn't know where he was, was summoned to the Gestapo-- Gestapo headquarters. And they said, we have to tell you, as a matter of factly, that your husband died of a heart attack. And then they gave her a cigar box. They said, here's is-- here are his ashes. And when my mother cried out, they said, look. You're taking-- you're-- you're robbing us of our time. Don't carry on here. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your mother's apartment been raided?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they came. They just wanted to know where my father was and where I was. They said to my mother, unless they present themselves immediately, we'll ransack the whole place. They didn't. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 10 days later, Goebbels called off the pogrom. I went home, back to Breslau from Berlin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, as a matter of fact, while I was in Berlin, I prepared my-- for my immigration. I found a travel agency in Berlin-- the Palestine [INAUDIBLE], whose manager said, I can get you out of Germany. But I need your passport, and you have to get a visa for Peru. I'll ship your passport to the Peruvian Consul in Paris. And for $280-- payable in dollars-- you will get a Peruvian visa, and we'll put you on a boat in Hamburg. I didn't have $280. It was under severe penalty-- it was prohibited in Germany for any German to own foreign currency. I had a very distant cousin in Gastonia, North Carolina, who had immigrated to the United States. He sent $280 to Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was an accountant with this-- with a textile mill in North Carolina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't we stop right here and change tapes?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. All right. I need to-- I'll be right back.
